content.text: Agentic AI is not merely a technological shift; it is an economic event of the first order. Systems that can autonomously execute knowledge work — researching, coding, writing, analysing, communicating, and managing sub-tasks across extended horizons — are entering a labour market that has spent two centuries absorbing waves of automation, but never one that directly targets the cognitive skills that previous waves displaced workers into. Understanding the economic impact requires distinguishing between substitution, complementarity, transition costs, and distributional effects, and then grounding those abstract categories in the specific task-level mechanisms through which agentic AI actually changes production functions.
What it is
Partial vs. full task automation
The relevant unit of analysis is the
task

, not the job. Acemoglu and Restrepo's task model (2018, 2022) frames automation as the displacement of specific tasks from human to machine, followed by the potential creation of new tasks that employ the displaced workers. Agentic AI is unusual in that it targets tasks distributed across the skill spectrum simultaneously — both the clerical tasks (writing summaries, answering emails) and the knowledge tasks (analysing contracts, writing code) that were previously safe from automation because they required general reasoning.
The distinction between
partial automation
(AI handles a subtask, human handles the rest) and
full automation

(AI handles the entire task end-to-end with no human-in-the-loop) is critical. Most current deployments are partial: an agent drafts, a human reviews. Full automation is already occurring at scale in narrow domains (customer service L1, document classification, code review). The economic impact of partial automation is primarily
augmentation
— one human does more; the impact of full automation is
substitution
— fewer humans are needed.
Baumol's cost disease and agentic AI

William Baumol's cost disease describes why labour-intensive sectors with low productivity growth (healthcare, education, performing arts) see costs rise over time relative to sectors with high productivity growth (manufacturing, software). Agentic AI, by dramatically raising labour productivity in knowledge-intensive services, may reverse cost disease in those sectors — doctors augmented by AI diagnostic agents, teachers augmented by personalised tutoring agents, lawyers augmented by document review agents. This would be historically unprecedented: the first technology to systematically attack the productivity stagnation of the service sector.
Why it matters

The scale of potential impact is large even under conservative assumptions. McKinsey (2023) estimated that generative AI could automate 60-70% of time spent on current tasks in many occupations; Goldman Sachs (2023) estimated that AI could expose 300 million full-time equivalent jobs globally to automation. These figures are contested and methodologically varied, but they share the direction: the affected share of labour is larger than for previous automation waves, and it concentrates in the highly-educated, highly-paid sectors that previous waves left untouched.

This matters for practitioners because the economic consequences of the systems they build are not externalities — they are foreseeable outcomes of architectural choices. An agent designed to fully automate customer service interactions, versus one designed to augment a human agent, produces different distributional outcomes at scale.
Architecture: Sector Impact Matrix

attrs.alt: Sector Impact Matrix showing automation disruption across white-collar vs blue-collar and routine vs novel tasks

content.text: The matrix above organises sectors by two dimensions: worker type (white-collar vs. blue-collar) and task type (routine vs. novel/creative). The impact of agentic AI is highest in the white-collar routine quadrant, where language-based, repetitive knowledge tasks are most amenable to LLM-driven agents. Blue-collar novel tasks (trades, caregiving) require physical dexterity and embodied judgment that current AI lacks.
Benefits

Productivity gains from agentic augmentation can reduce the cost of expert services — legal advice, medical second opinions, financial planning — that are currently inaccessible to lower-income individuals.
Firms that successfully integrate agentic AI into high-value workflows see compounding competitive advantages: faster iteration, lower marginal cost per unit of output.

In developing economies, agentic AI may leapfrog institutional constraints: an AI-assisted healthcare worker in a low-resource setting can deliver diagnostic support that would otherwise require years of specialist training.

GDP growth projections from augmentation are significant: PricewaterhouseCoopers estimated that AI could add 15.7 trillion USD to global GDP by 2030, largely through productivity gains in knowledge work.

New task creation — the mechanism Acemoglu and Restrepo identify as the historical offset to automation displacement — could accelerate: agentic AI infrastructure itself generates demand for prompt engineers, agent orchestrators, AI auditors, and alignment researchers.
Trade-offs

Distributional inequality: productivity gains accrue primarily to capital owners and high-skilled workers who direct AI systems, while displacement costs fall on mid-skill workers who cannot easily retrain.

Transition speed mismatch: labour markets adjust to automation over decades; agentic AI capabilities are advancing on a timescale of years. The retraining systems and social safety nets are structurally slower than the disruption.

Hollowing-out risk: if agentic AI displaces mid-tier knowledge workers but the new tasks created are mostly high-expertise positions, the middle of the income distribution shrinks further — accelerating the polarisation trend documented since the 1980s.

Market concentration: the infrastructure for agentic AI (compute, foundation models, agent frameworks) is highly concentrated in a small number of firms. Productivity gains captured by these firms increase market power and may enable monopolistic pricing in downstream markets.

Creative industry disruption: generative and agentic AI puts downward pressure on wages in creative fields (writing, illustration, music composition) without proportional new task creation in those fields.
How it works
Substitution vs. complementarity

The substitution effect and the complementarity effect operate simultaneously, and which dominates determines whether a worker's labour is worth more or less after AI deployment. Substitution dominates when the AI can perform the task to a sufficient standard at lower marginal cost than the worker. Complementarity dominates when the AI enhances the worker's productivity on tasks that remain human-critical — judgment, client relationship management, novel problem framing, ethical reasoning under uncertainty.

The economic literature on previous automation waves (Autor, Levy, Murnane 2003; Autor 2015) consistently found that routine-task automation was substitutive while non-routine task automation was complementary. Agentic AI complicates this pattern because it attacks non-routine cognitive tasks for the first time — legal reasoning, scientific analysis, software architecture — that previous frameworks treated as automation-resistant. The complementarity effect still applies but is narrowing in scope to tasks requiring embodied judgment, interpersonal trust, and genuine novelty.
Production function transformation

Formally, if a firm's production function is Q = f(K, L, A) where K is capital, L is labour, and A is AI capability, then agentic AI shifts the effective labour composition. Some tasks previously requiring L now require only A (substitution). Others become output of L·A where the product is superlinear in A — each additional unit of AI capability multiplies the worker's effective output (complementarity). The aggregate effect on total labour demand depends on the elasticity of demand for the firm's output: if cheaper production induces significantly more demand (elastic market), total employment can rise even with substitution.
Mental model

The economic impact of agentic AI is structurally similar to the impact of the mechanical loom on textile workers — but happening simultaneously across dozens of industries that span the education spectrum. The key difference is speed and breadth. The textile industry's displacement happened over a generation; agentic AI's displacement may happen over a decade. And where mechanisation targeted physical labour, leaving cognitive work as the refuge, agentic AI targets cognitive labour, leaving only the most embodied, relational, or genuinely novel work as the refuge.
When to use it
Economic impact analysis is relevant to practitioners in three decision contexts:
Product design: deciding whether to design for augmentation (human-in-the-loop) or substitution (fully automated), with awareness of the distributional consequences of each choice.
Organisational deployment: modelling the task-level impact of agent deployment on team structure, role definitions, and compensation before rolling out to production.

Policy engagement: providing technically accurate input to policymakers about which tasks are automatable, at what timescale, and what complementary interventions (retraining, wage insurance, UBI pilots) would mitigate displacement.
Investment decisions: forecasting which sectors will see the largest productivity gains and which will see employment contractions within a 3-5 year horizon.
How to implement: task automation scoring system

"""
Task automation scoring: estimate the automation probability and
substitution vs. complementarity ratio for a set of occupational tasks.

Inspired by Frey & Osborne (2017) and Acemoglu-Restrepo (2022),
adapted for agentic AI capabilities.
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal
import json


@dataclass
class Task:
    name: str
    requires_physical_dexterity: float    # 0-1
    requires_novel_problem_framing: float # 0-1
    requires_interpersonal_trust: float   # 0-1
    is_language_based: float              # 0-1
    is_routine_procedure: float           # 0-1
    requires_realtime_embodied_context: float  # 0-1
    output_verifiability: float           # 0-1: can output be checked easily?


def automation_score(task: Task) -> float:
    """
    Returns 0-1 probability of full automation within 5 years.
    Higher = more automatable.
    """
    # Facilitators of automation (agentic AI excels at these)
    score = (
        task.is_language_based * 0.30 +
        task.is_routine_procedure * 0.25 +
        task.output_verifiability * 0.20
    )
    # Inhibitors of automation (agentic AI currently struggles)
    score -= (
        task.requires_physical_dexterity * 0.25 +
        task.requires_novel_problem_framing * 0.20 +
        task.requires_interpersonal_trust * 0.15 +
        task.requires_realtime_embodied_context * 0.20
    )
    return max(0.0, min(1.0, score + 0.5))  # centre at 0.5


def impact_category(score: float) -> str:
    if score >= 0.75: return "HIGH substitution risk"
    if score >= 0.50: return "MEDIUM disruption / mixed"
    if score >= 0.30: return "LOW risk — complementarity dominant"
    return "VERY LOW risk — safe for decade+"


# ---- Example task library ---------------------------------------------------

TASKS = [
    Task("Legal document review",
         requires_physical_dexterity=0.0,
         requires_novel_problem_framing=0.2,
         requires_interpersonal_trust=0.1,
         is_language_based=1.0,
         is_routine_procedure=0.8,
         requires_realtime_embodied_context=0.0,
         output_verifiability=0.9),

    Task("Software debugging (known patterns)",
         requires_physical_dexterity=0.0,
         requires_novel_problem_framing=0.2,
         requires_interpersonal_trust=0.0,
         is_language_based=0.8,
         is_routine_procedure=0.7,
         requires_realtime_embodied_context=0.0,
         output_verifiability=0.95),

    Task("Strategic consulting (client-facing)",
         requires_physical_dexterity=0.0,
         requires_novel_problem_framing=0.9,
         requires_interpersonal_trust=0.9,
         is_language_based=0.8,
         is_routine_procedure=0.1,
         requires_realtime_embodied_context=0.4,
         output_verifiability=0.3),

    Task("Nursing (hospital bedside)",
         requires_physical_dexterity=0.9,
         requires_novel_problem_framing=0.5,
         requires_interpersonal_trust=0.9,
         is_language_based=0.4,
         is_routine_procedure=0.4,
         requires_realtime_embodied_context=1.0,
         output_verifiability=0.7),

    Task("Customer support email (L1)",
         requires_physical_dexterity=0.0,
         requires_novel_problem_framing=0.1,
         requires_interpersonal_trust=0.3,
         is_language_based=1.0,
         is_routine_procedure=0.9,
         requires_realtime_embodied_context=0.0,
         output_verifiability=0.85),
]


for task in TASKS:
    s = automation_score(task)
    print(f"{task.name:<40s}  score={s:.2f}  [{impact_category(s)}]")
Examples
Economic impact modelling at firm level

"""
Firm-level economic impact model: estimate productivity gain and
headcount change from agent deployment across a task portfolio.
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Occupation:
    title: str
    annual_salary_usd: int
    headcount: int
    tasks: list[Task]

def model_firm_impact(occupations: list[Occupation]) -> dict:
    total_wage_bill = 0
    total_productivity_gain = 0.0
    at_risk_fte = 0.0

    for occ in occupations:
        # Average automation score across tasks
        scores = [automation_score(t) for t in occ.tasks]
        avg_score = sum(scores) / len(scores)

        # Substitution fraction: high-score tasks become automated
        substitution_fraction = sum(s for s in scores if s > 0.6) / len(scores)

        # Complementarity multiplier on remaining tasks
        complement_fraction = 1.0 - substitution_fraction
        productivity_multiplier = 1.0 + complement_fraction * 0.4  # 40% gain on non-automated tasks

        wage_bill = occ.headcount * occ.annual_salary_usd
        savings = wage_bill * substitution_fraction  # potential labour cost reduction
        gain = wage_bill * complement_fraction * 0.4  # productivity gain value

        total_wage_bill += wage_bill
        total_productivity_gain += gain + savings
        at_risk_fte += occ.headcount * substitution_fraction

        print(f"  {occ.title:<35s} subst={substitution_fraction:.1%}  "
              f"productivity_gain=${gain/1e6:.1f}M  at_risk={occ.headcount * substitution_fraction:.0f} FTE")

    roi_pct = total_productivity_gain / total_wage_bill * 100
    print(f"\nTotal at-risk FTE: {at_risk_fte:.0f}")
    print(f"Total annual productivity gain: ${total_productivity_gain/1e6:.1f}M")
    print(f"ROI vs. wage bill: {roi_pct:.1f}%")
    return {"at_risk_fte": at_risk_fte, "productivity_gain_usd": total_productivity_gain}
Real-world example
Legal sector: contract review automation

Large law firms are deploying agentic AI to handle first-pass contract review, due diligence in M&A transactions, and regulatory compliance screening. Firms like Linklaters (Harvey AI partnership) and Allen & Overy report that first-year associates spend 40-60% of their time on tasks that are now partially or fully automatable by agentic systems. The economic impact is dual: billable hours from associates decline (substitution), but partner-level work on complex strategy and client advisory becomes more valuable and time-efficient (complementarity). The distributional consequence is that entry-level associate hiring may contract, compressing the pipeline for senior lawyer development and changing how the profession trains its next generation.
Common mistakes

Treating 'job automation' and 'task automation' as equivalent. Jobs that are 70% automatable by task do not disappear — they transform. Only jobs where nearly all constituent tasks are automatable face near-term elimination.

Ignoring Baumol complementarity: sectors that become dramatically cheaper due to AI (legal services, software) may see demand increase so substantially that employment in those sectors grows rather than shrinks.
Assuming uniform global impact. Developing economies with younger workforces and weaker institutional protection may face different disruption timelines than advanced economies.

Conflating productivity gain with welfare gain. An economy where 10% of the population captures 90% of the productivity gains from AI is a welfare loss for most people, even if GDP statistics look positive.

Ignoring second-order effects: automating knowledge work reduces the labour market value of the degrees and credentials that knowledge workers hold, changing educational investment decisions decades before the displaced workers would naturally retire.
Best practices
Before deploying agents in a new domain, conduct a task-level audit of the roles affected using frameworks like the O*NET database or similar occupational task inventories.
Design for augmentation as the default and full automation as the exception. The transition path for workers is smoother when humans remain in the loop during the capability build-up phase.

Monitor leading indicators of displacement: in agentic AI contexts these include: time-to-complete on templated tasks (falling → substitution imminent), volume of human-reviewed outputs (falling → quality high enough for full automation).

Engage with workforce transition planning early. Firms that announce agent deployments without transition support face legal risk (in jurisdictions with consultation requirements) and reputational risk.

Participate in industry-level coordination on reskilling: the externalities of labour displacement are diffuse and no single firm has the incentive to internalise them, so collective action through industry bodies is needed.
Performance and scalability

The economic impact scales super-linearly with agent capability. A modest improvement in base model quality (e.g., from a 70% task completion rate to 80%) translates into a much larger expansion of the automatable task frontier, because the marginal tasks that move from 'requires human review' to 'fully automated' tend to be higher-volume. Organisations should model their agent capability pipeline as an economic forecast: each model generation upgrade has a quantifiable expected effect on the automation fraction and should be reflected in workforce planning.
Tooling and ecosystem
O*NET Database (US Bureau of Labor Statistics): task-level occupational data covering 900+ occupations, usable for automation risk assessment.
World Economic Forum Future of Jobs Report: annual survey of employer expectations for automation-driven job displacement and creation across sectors.
MIT Work of the Future research group: empirically grounded analysis of automation's distributional effects in the US labour market.
OECD Employment Outlook: cross-country data on automation risk by occupation, enabling international comparison.
Upwork Economic Research: real-time labour market data on demand shifts for AI-adjacent skills vs. traditional knowledge work categories.
Comparison with alternatives
Automation wave
Tasks targeted
Displacement timescale
Key refuge for workers
Industrial revolution
Physical routine (weaving, milling)
Cognitive work, services
Software / ERP (1980s-2000s)
Routine cognitive (bookkeeping, data entry)
~2 decades
Non-routine cognitive, interpersonal
Narrow ML (2010s)
Perceptual tasks (image classification)
~1 decade
General reasoning, language
Agentic AI (2023+)
Non-routine cognitive (research, code, law)
Embodied, relational, genuinely novel
Security and reliability

The economic risks of agentic AI deployment include both direct financial risks (agents making costly errors in financial or legal workflows) and systemic risks (over-reliance on a small number of agent infrastructure providers creating single points of failure in economic critical systems). Organisations should model their agent dependency the way they model supply chain concentration: what is the business impact if the agent infrastructure provider goes offline, changes pricing, or discontinues a model?

Regulatory risk is also material. Jurisdictions with labour consultation requirements may require advance notification before agent deployments that materially reduce headcount. Failure to comply creates employment law liability, particularly in the EU under the EU AI Act and Collective Redundancies Directive.
Key takeaways
Agentic AI attacks non-routine cognitive tasks for the first time in the history of automation, targeting the refuge that previous automation waves left open.
The economic effect is task-level, not job-level: most jobs are portfolios of automatable and non-automatable tasks, and the mix determines net employment impact.
Substitution and complementarity operate simultaneously; which dominates depends on task novelty, interpersonal content, and output verifiability.
Productivity gains from AI could reverse Baumol's cost disease in knowledge-intensive services, making expert services dramatically more accessible — if the gains are distributed broadly.
Transition speed mismatch is the core policy problem: AI capability advances in years, but labour market adjustment systems operate on decades.
Market concentration in AI infrastructure creates distributional risk: gains may accrue disproportionately to a small number of firms and their shareholders.
Practitioners have ethical responsibility to model and anticipate the displacement effects of the agentic systems they design, not to treat them as externalities.
